ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general kebab food product class; shish kebab (skewered grilled marinated meat) is a specific variant. 'closest_parent' match used as no exact shish kebab URI confirmed in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated shish kebab code. A0FJP (grilled meat product) is the closest defensible anchor. 'closest_parent' match used as exact shish kebab code not confirmed in FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code confirmed for shish kebab as a specific food concept as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 300g including meat, salad, sauce, and rice or bread)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of shish kebab is approximately 300g, comprising grilled skewered meat (lamb or chicken), a side salad, sauce, and a choice of butter rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Shish Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Shish Kebab (cr_id G00010176), confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/chicken kebab analogues as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat and rice/bread dish with low carbohydrate fraction
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shish kebab as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the following reasoning: the dish is predominantly protein and fat (meat component) with a small carbohydrate fraction (5.24g/100g) from marinade sugars and accompaniments. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 40–55 for mixed meat-and-rice dishes and grilled meat with bread. A conservative GI of 45 is applied. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (45 × 5.24 × 3) / 100 = 7.1g, classified as low GL. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080mg per 100g — well above the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit shish kebab due to its very high sodium content (1080mg/100g); if consumed, request reduced-salt marinade, avoid additional dipping sauces, and limit portion to no more than 100g to keep sodium contribution below 1100mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24g/100g) and low sugar (2.85g/100g) are favourable, but high total fat (26.4g/100g) and high sodium (1080mg/100g) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Shish kebab has a low carbohydrate content and estimated low GI (≈45), making it broadly compatible with a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan; however, the high fat content (26.4g/100g) contributes to caloric density and should be managed with moderate portion sizes (≤150g meat), and the bread or rice accompaniment should be substituted with a low-GI option or omitted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~10.2g/100g), high sodium (1080mg/100g), and cholesterol (85mg/100g) are collectively adverse for cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit shish kebab consumption due to its high saturated fat (~10.2g/100g), high sodium (1080mg/100g), and cholesterol (85mg/100g); if consumed, choose the chicken variant over lamb, request reduced-salt preparation, limit portion to 100g, and avoid high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb/beef) provides arachidonic acid; high sodium (1080mg/100g) worsens tissue inflammation; high-heat grilling may generate pro-inflammatory compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the lamb or beef variant of shish kebab due to pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content and high sodium; substituting with the chicken variant and requesting reduced-salt marinade may reduce inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shish kebab is a Middle Eastern grilled meat dish consisting of boneless pieces of marinated meat — typically lamb or chicken — threaded onto skewers and cooked over an open flame or grill. The meat is marinated in a blend of herbs and spices before grilling, imparting a smoky, savoury flavour with a slightly charred exterior and tender interior. In the Singapore context (HPB Foodo entry G00010176), shish kebab is served as a complete plate meal with homemade sauce, a side salad, and a choice of butter rice or bread. The chicken variant is served with tomato gravy, while the lamb variant is served with brown sauce (which contains beef). Per 100g, the dish provides 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, 26.4g fat, 5.24g carbohydrate, 2.85g sugar, 0.0g dietary fibre, and 1080mg sodium. The high sodium content (1080mg/100g) makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycemic index (~45) make it broadly compatible with Type 2 Diabetes management when portion sizes are controlled and high-GI accompaniments are avoided. The dish is a good source of potassium (387mg/100g), calcium (118mg/100g), and iron (1.15mg/100g). Shish kebab is widely consumed across Middle Eastern, Turkish, and Mediterranean cuisines and is available in Singapore as a restaurant and hawker-adjacent dish.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in shish kebab?
Is shish kebab suitable for hypertension?
What is the calorie count of shish kebab?

What is the difference between shish chicken and shish lamb?
What is the GI of shish kebab?
How many calories in a plate of shish kebab?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010176) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues for lamb/chicken kebab. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database mixed meat-dish references. All primary macros and secondary nutrients are from an approved primary source.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB Foodo for this dish; this is plausible given the meat-dominant composition and the fibre contribution of salad and sauce components being minimal or not separately reported. Confirmed zero per HPB source. 2. saturated_fat_g (10.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb kebab and chicken kebab analogues (USDA SR Legacy entries for grilled lamb and grilled chicken), as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA analogue data not applied to avoid compounding estimation error. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no approved-source analogue was applied to avoid unverified estimation. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific dish preparation is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish may contain beef (brown sauce) and the halal certification of the specific restaurant preparation is unknown. 7. The input health_suitability field referenced trans_fat_g of 0.2g/100g for the rheumatoid arthritis caution; this value is not confirmed by HPB Foodo and has not been carried forward. trans_fat_g is set to -1 pending a confirmed source.
